policies.title: Aggregation and Allocation Policy
policies.summary: Aggregation and Allocation Policy The Firm’s obligation The aggregation and allocation of client orders must be effected in a manner which is fair and equitable to all clients.
policies.content:
Aggregation and Allocation Policy

Aggregation and Allocation Policy The Firm’s obligation The aggregation and allocation of client orders must be effected in a manner which is fair and equitable to all clients.

Aggregation The Firm may aggregate a client order with those of other clients (and clients of affiliates of the Firm) and its own account from time to time for the efficient execution of orders for the benefit of all clients. The Firm will endeavour not to carry out a client order or a transaction in aggregation with another order if it is likely that the aggregation of orders will work to the disadvantage of any client whose order is to be aggregated. In the event that an aggregation would disadvantage a client, it must be disclosed to each client whose order may be aggregated, that the effect of aggregation may work to its disadvantage in relation to a particular order. The Firm will take into account the investment strategy and risk profile of each client before allocating aggregated orders. Client orders that are aggregated with an order for the Firm’s own account will be allocated with priority to the Client(s) at all times.

Allocation When allocating orders across funds or client accounts, the following methodologies and factors will be considered: •

Pro rata;

•

Pari passu;

•

Volume of the order;

•

Capacity of the relevant portfolio in line with investment restrictions;

•

Time horizons; and

•

Price of the order.

Generally, allocations among clients with the same or a similar investment objective should be pro-rata based on the relative market values of the aggregated clients’ portfolios. Where the aggregated orders are executed in differently sized tranches and at different prices, the allocation will be made at the weighted average price achieved. If an order is partially executed, the subsequent related orders required to complete the original aggregated order must be allocated on the same basis as the original order. Deviations from the general rule may be permitted in the following specific circumstances where allocations may be based on: •

Existing positions in the client accounts:

•

Different risk limits where the use of pro-rata based on portfolio assets may mean a client exceeds a risk limit;

•

The availability of cash in the particular accounts;

•

Tax reasons; and

•

Shares to be allocated to the largest account if the securities traded are too small to be reasonably allocated to all clients.

Reallocation of a transaction following its allocation must be pre-approved by the Chief Compliance Officer.

Cross Trades In the rare instance that the Firm transfers securities between client portfolios (“cross trades”), pre-approval / input / oversight/ by the Chief Compliance Officer must be sought to ensure that such transfers are beneficial to both clients and are executed at a fair price. Upon approval the Chief Compliance Officer will generate a cross report replete with a detailed rationale, execution specifics and a separate TCA report. This is subject to review by the CEO. Allocation is monitored on an on-going basis to ensure compliance with the Firm’s policy.

Record Keeping The Firm maintains a record of allocations by client and updates the records where there is a re-allocation.

Policy Review The Chief Compliance Officer will be responsible for reviewing this policy annually (or at the request of the CEO) to ensure that it continues to meet legal requirements and that it reflects best business practice.

policies.title: Cash Controls Policy
policies.summary: Cash Controls Policy Scope of Policy This Policy governs the management, safeguarding, and reconciliation of investment vehicle cash. The Firm does not hold client money or client assets within the meaning of the FCA CASS rules. It applies only to proprietary and operational cash
policies.content:
Cash Controls Policy

Cash Controls Policy Scope of Policy This Policy governs the management, safeguarding, and reconciliation of investment vehicle cash. The Firm does not hold client money or client assets within the meaning of the FCA CASS rules. It applies only to proprietary and operational cash balances, including bank accounts, custody accounts, and prime brokerage accounts. The Policy integrates both the Firm’s internal dual-control procedures and the external authorisation framework established with its prime brokers..

Regulatory Context The policy aligns with FCA rules ( SYSC, MiFIDPRU), SMCR requirements, and AML/CTF legislation. It is reviewed annually by Compliance and approved by the Board. 

SYSC / MiFIDPRU: Internal systems and controls over financial resources, risk, and governance.



SMCR: Accountability of the CFO/Finance Partner and COO/Operations Partner.



AML/CTF / POCA: All payments subject to AML screening and dual sign-off.

Principles
1. Dual Approval – No cash movement may be effected without two authorised signatories, one of whom must be
a Partner/Designated Member.
2. Segregation – Proprietary cash and operational cash are segregated, with no client assets held.
3. Daily Reconciliation – All balances are reconciled against external statements.
4. Escalation – Breaks, exceptions or overdrafts are escalated immediately.
5. Audit Trail – All approvals and reconciliations are retained for seven years.

External Authorisation Framework
The Firm adopts a structured methodology, aligned with broker documentation to govern external payment
instructions:
• Cash Payment Authorisations (CPA/CPSTA): Formal instructions are lodged with each prime broker or custodian,
setting out who may instruct payments or transfers.
• Authorised Signatory Schedule: The Firm maintains an up-to-date list of authorised individuals, with role-based
limitations. Initiators may not act as approvers.
• Legal Framework: All external authorisation arrangements are governed by English law and subject to the
jurisdiction of English courts.
• Supersession: Any new instruction schedules replace and supersede prior versions, ensuring clarity.
• Enforceability: All obligations arising under these authorisations are interpreted in accordance with English law.

Payment Procedures

Payments must comply with the following process:
• Initiation – Prepared by Finance/Operations.
• Verification – Supported by invoice, trade confirmation, or collateral call.
• Approval – Requires dual authorisation, including one Partner/Designated Member.
• Execution – Only individuals listed on the authorised external schedule may instruct the broker/custodian.
• AML/CTF – All counterparties are subject to AML and sanctions screening.

Reconciliation and Monitoring Operations conduct daily reconciliations of all accounts. Finance performs monthly reviews of reconciliations. Unreconciled items above defined thresholds are escalated immediately to the COO. Quarterly reports are presented to the LLP ExCo.

Access and Systems Controls Access to online banking and custody platforms is restricted to authorised personnel via MFA only. Dual authentication is mandatory. User access rights are reviewed quarterly. Passwords and security credentials are rotated in line with the Firm’s ISP.

Governance and Oversight The SMF16/17 partner has accountability for financial oversight. The COO is responsible for daily operation of controls. The Board receives quarterly Cash Management Reports. All breaches of this Policy are escalated to the COO/COO. Material breaches are reported to the FCA under Principle 11.

Training and Record Keeping Staff with cash-handling responsibilities must undergo annual training. Payment instructions, reconciliations, and approvals are retained for seven years in compliance with GDPR.

Integration Note This Policy unifies the Firm’s internal control environment (dual approval, segregation, reconciliation) with its external legal and operational framework (broker Cash Payment Authorisation methodology). Together these ensure that all cash movements are internally controlled, externally authorised, and executed in compliance with FCA expectations and English law.

policies.content:
Conflicts of Interest Policy

Conflicts of Interest Policy Purpose

The purpose of this Conflicts of Interest Policy under SYSC 10.1.11R is: •

To identify, by reference to the specific services and activities carried out by (or on behalf of) the Firm, the circumstances which constitute or may give rise to a conflict of interest entailing a material risk of damage to the interests of one or more clients; and

•

To specify procedures to be followed and measures to be adopted in order to manage such conflicts; and

•

To communicate this information to all those who are in the Firm.

It is the responsibility of all staff members to familiarise themselves both with the contents of the Policy and the ‘Conflicts of Interest’ section of this manual (see ‘Senior Management Arrangements and Systems and Controls’) and report conflicts of interest to the Compliance Officer using the appropriate channels.

Regulatory Background

FCA Principle 8 (Conflicts of Interest) states that: A firm must manage conflicts of interest fairly, both between itself and its customers and between a customer and another customer. These requirements have been amplified in the FCA sourcebook SYSC (Senior Management Arrangements, Systems and Controls). The SYSC organisational requirements is designed to clarify what is expected of firms and of their senior management. It does this by having management oversight and systems and controls in place in several areas, including conflicts of interest.

Summary of Requirements

1. Identifying Conflicts
SYSC 10.1.3R requires a firm to take all appropriate steps to identify conflicts of interest between:
1. The Firm (including its managers, employees, tied agents/appointed representatives or any
person directly or indirectly linked to them by control) and a client of the firm; or
2. One client of the firm and another client.
That arise or may arise in the course of the firm providing any services in the course of carrying on regulated
activities.
Where relevant, reference to ‘client’ can be read to also include ‘fund’ in this Appendix.
1. Types of Conflict
SYSC 10.1.4R sets out that, for the purpose of identifying the types of conflict that arise in the course of
providing a service, and, where there may be a material risk of damage to the interests of a client, the Firm
must take into account certain issues. It must consider as a minimum, whether the Firm or a relevant person
or a person directly or indirectly linked by control to the Firm:
1. Is likely to make a financial gain or avoid a financial loss at the expense of the client;
2. Has an interest in the outcome of a service provided to the client or of a transaction carried out on behalf
of the client, which is distinct from the client’s interest in that outcome;
3. Has a financial or other incentive to favour the interest of another client or group of clients over the
interests of the client;
4. Carries on the same business as the client;
5. Receives or will receive from a person other than the client an inducement in relation to a service provided
to the client, in the form of monies, goods or services- other than the standard commission or fee for that
service.
2. Segregation of functions

SYSC 5.1.6 to 5.1.11 require the senior management of a firm to segregate duties so as to avoid conflicts of
interest.
3. Disclosure of conflicts to clients
SYSC 10.1.8R requires that, where the arrangements made by the Firm are not sufficient to ensure with
reasonable confidence that the risk of damage to the client will be prevented, the Firm must clearly disclose,
in a durable medium, the general nature and sources of the conflicts of interest to the client and the steps
taken to mitigate those risks before undertaking business for the client.
The disclosure must:
1. clearly state that the organisational and administrative arrangements established by the Firm to
prevent or manage that conflict are not sufficient to ensure, with reasonable confidence, that the risks
of damage to the interests of the client will be prevented;
2. include specific description of the conflicts of interest that arise in the provision of investment services
or ancillary services;
3. explain the risks to the client that arise as a result of the conflicts of interest; and
4. include sufficient detail, taking into account the nature of the client, to enable that client to take an
informed decision with respect to the service in the context of which the conflict of interest arises.
5. Responsibilities of Staff Members
It is the responsibility of all employees to familiarise themselves with this Policy and to report conflicts of
interest to their line manager who will in turn report them to the Compliance Officer. Failure to adhere to
this policy may be held to be a breach of an employee’s contract.
Overall responsibility for Conflicts of Interest lies with the Governing Body. The Compliance Officer is
responsible for the day-to-day administration of the Policy.
The Compliance Officer will work with line management to eliminate Conflicts of Interest, record conflicts
and the mitigating action in the Conflicts Register and report the situation to the for consideration.
The Governing Body, via the Compliance Officer, has responsibility for ensuring that staff are aware of the
aspects of the Policy relevant to them.
All staff members have a responsibility for carrying out aspects of the policy that are relevant to them.

Situations in which Conflicts of Interest could arise

The purpose of this Section is to set out typical situations in which conflicts of interest arise and are managed in the course of the Firm’s day-to-day business, so that employees are better equipped to identify, report and assist in eliminating or managing conflicts. a. Potential misuse of information Potential conflict Members of staff of the Firm may come into possession of material non-public information. The improper use of such information by staff members could cause a conflict with the interests of the Firm’s clients, or between the interests of the Firm’s clients, and may also be unlawful. Method of managing/avoiding conflict The Firm manages these risks by maintaining and following policies and procedures to prevent the misuse of material non-public information. These procedures have been designed to prevent and detect any insider trading, taking into account the nature of the Firm’s business and the instruments typically traded. The Firm has also implemented procedures to manage the risks of Insider Dealing, including using the Compliance Department as a “Gatekeeper” to clear any Inside Information based conflicts, the use of restricted/Insider Lists and deal monitoring. b. Personal Account Dealing (“PAD”) Potential conflict The Firm’s staff members may engage in the trading of securities or other instruments for their own account. Such trading activities may put those employees and officers, or the Firm, in conflict with the interests of the Firm’s clients (for example, by having a personal interest in a transaction with a client, or by front-running transactions with clients). Method of managing/avoiding conflict The Firm manages this potential conflict of interest by maintaining a ‘PAD’ Policy which has been formulated in accordance with relevant FCA Rules. All staff members must seek prior permission from the

Compliance Officer to deal in securities for their own account. Each Director and employee is responsible for checking the Restricted List prior to dealing. In addition, the Compliance Officer will monitor all PAD against the Insider List. c. Inducements Potential conflict The giving or receiving of gifts, entertainment, or any other form of gratuity or hospitality by or to the Firm’s staff members may create the appearance of a lack of impartiality and may lead to a potential conflict between the interests of the donor/donee and the interests of the clients. Method of managing/avoiding conflict Staff members are prohibited from giving to and accepting from clients, potential clients, or other third parties’ gifts and entertainment of above the specified threshold. d. Remuneration Policy/Performance Fees Potential conflict A conflict may arise in respect of the Firm’s fee-based income from its AUM. The Firm will ensure that all investment holding valuations are conducted on an “arms-length” basis by a fund administrator of good repute and in accordance with the valuation policy applicable to the relevant fund. Method of managing/avoiding conflict Potential conflicts arising and arrangements for controlling/mitigating them are identified in the Conflicts of Interest Register. Firm and employee interests are aligned with those of the Firm’s clients. e. Outside Business Interests Potential conflict The Firm’s staff members may hold outside business interests, such as directorships or shareholdings, in service providers or other firms. The Firm has identified that such outside business interests or

investments could cause a potential conflict between the personal interest of the relevant member of staff and the interests of the Firm’s clients.

Method of managing/avoiding conflict Staff members must inform the Compliance Officer about their outside business interests. The Compliance Officer must approve any such interests and will maintain a record of them. f.

Introduction Fees Potential conflict From time to time, the Firm may pay introduction fees to third parties in relation to executed transactions. Method of managing/avoiding conflict In such cases, full disclosure of the existence of the introduction fee is be provided to the client.

g. Group Conflicts Potential conflict Other members of the Alamut Group may have client relationships that could create a conflict of interest. Method of managing/avoiding conflict The Firm maintains global conflicts register and insider list which captures all actual and potential conflicts that may arise or inside information obtained. h. Personal Interests in Funds The potential conflict may be that a staff member receives an economic or other benefit compared to third party investors.

Full disclosure of the Directors’ and employees’ investing terms is made to all existing investors. The Firm believes that staff members investing in its Fund(s) is likely to align their interest with that of third party investors, rather than create conflict. i.

Staff on notice to leave Conflicts of interest could arise if a member of staff on notice to leave the Firm concentrates on higher risk short term performance in order to achieve a larger leaving bonus. The Governing Body of the Firm monitors the risk level of investments and, where necessary, gives instructions to restore the risk balance. If an employee or Director on notice is seen to pose a particular risk, then that individual will be put on ‘gardening leave’ to remove the possibility of inappropriate trading.

j.

Disparity between performance fees for different funds or portfolios The potential conflict is in a situation where the Firm concentrates on a particular client or fund that is paying higher fees than another client or fund. The funds’ fee arrangements are fully disclosed to all actual and potential investors. In addition, the Firm ensures that it is sufficiently resourced to be in a position to provide as many resources as necessary to discharge its management obligation for each client.

k. Public statements Potential conflict and Market Abuse issues arise if the portfolio managers make public statements in order to “talk up or down” a particular security in which the portfolios managed by the Firm have a position. The Firm and its staff do not currently make public statements about the Firm or its investments. The Firm also has procedures in place whereby employees are not allowed to talk to the press regarding the Firm and its investments unless prior approvals from the Compliance Officer and the Governing Body of the Firm have been received (see also ‘Rumours Policy’ in these Appendices). l.

Trade and Initial Public Offering allocations Conflicts may arise where the Firm only receives scaled back allocation for oversubscribed IPOs.

Such a conflict is managed in accordance with the Firm’s general allocation policy. Other conflicts may include over trading with a particular Broker to generate additional commissions for that Broker - in order to ensure the Firm is considered a “good” client for any IPO.

Arrangements for managing conflicts

1. Governance
1. The Firm has robust governance arrangements. Key business decisions are taken by the Governing
Body and are recorded.
2. The Compliance Officer reports directly to the Governing Body.
3. The Firm has rules in place to govern employee conduct, including PAD rules which control and
mitigate conflicts of interest. It also maintains a Conflicts of Interest Register.
2. Reporting Lines
The Firm has defined and clear reporting lines. An organisational chart is maintained by the Compliance
Officer.
3. Segregation of Functions
The rules in SYSC 5.1 requiring segregation of functions are met by segregating duties, as appropriate, to
avoid conflicts of interest wherever possible. These duties are set out in job descriptions, procedure
manuals and organisational charts. Ensuring these duties remain segregated is the responsibility of line
managers, as advised by the Compliance Officer.
4. Disclosure of Personal Conflicts
Employees and owners are required to disclose conflicts of interest. Employees will disclose any conflicts
of interest to their line manager, who in turn will inform the Compliance Officer. Owners will disclose any
conflicts directly to the Compliance Officer. The Compliance Officer will record in the appropriate register
and inform the Governing Body of any action taken.

5. Disclosure to Clients
If the Firm’s arrangements to manage a conflict of interest are not sufficient to ensure, with reasonable
confidence, that the risk of damage to that client’s interests is prevented, the Firm is required to inform
the client, in a durable medium, in order to allow them to take an informed decision before business is
undertaken.
6. Restricted List and Insider List
In order to facilitate the monitoring of conflicts, the Firm maintains a global Restricted List and an Insider
List.
7. Inducements
Staff members are prohibited from giving to and accepting from clients, potential clients or other third
parties, gifts and entertainment of above the specified threshold. The Firm also maintains a ‘Bribery’
Policy.
8. Recruitment
The fitness and propriety of potential new employees is considered by the Compliance Officer when they
are in the process of being recruited by the Firm. Their technical and, where relevant, managerial ability is
also considered. Suitable background checks are made and references are taken up.
9. Training
Compliance training regarding conflicts of interest forms part of the annual training needs analysis. The
Compliance Officer ensures that appropriate training is devised and delivered.
10. Compliance and Procedures Manuals
Systems and controls are documented in the compliance and procedures manuals which are reviewed at
least once a year to ensure they are fit for purpose. The reviewer is appointed by the Governing Body.

11. Periodic Audit
The Compliance Officer oversees and executes a suitable audit programme at least annually to verify that
the systems and controls are being applied.
12. Management Information
Management information regarding the identification of conflicts is reviewed by the Compliance Officer.
Conflicts checks are undertaken when taking on new clients or accepting new business from existing
clients.
13. Verifying Compliance
To verify that these policies have been complied with, an annual compliance review will be undertaken by
the Compliance Officer. The Compliance Officer will have responsibility for assessing compliance with the
policy on an annual basis and will report formally to the Governing Body.
14. Remuneration
The Firm’s remuneration policy is designed to avoid rewarding behaviour that could lead to disadvantage
for its clients. See ‘Remuneration’ Policy.
15. Conflicts Monitoring
Potential conflicts of interest are considered on an ongoing basis and prior to taking on a new client. In
cases where a conflict is identified, a decision is made as to whether to proceed with the new client and,
if so, what additional measures should be taken to mitigate the conflict. All such decisions are documented
and are based on the nature of the conflict and the potential for the conflict to entail a material risk of
damage to the interest of one or more clients. The Compliance Officer keeps records of business approval
and related correspondence.
16. Confidentiality
No portion of this Policy may be copied, reproduced, or shown to any individual who is not an employee
of the Firm, a representative of a relevant legal or regulatory authority or a relevant professional advisor.

17. Approval
This Policy was last amended in November 2024 and, following review, was approved by the Governing Body on
29th November 2024.

COMPLIANCE GUIDELINES: CONFLICTS OF INTEREST POLICY

Conflict Assessment and Scoring The Conflicts Inventory contains Alamut’s assessment of the materiality of each conflict identified. On the basis of this assessment, a determination is made as to the course of action required. As discussed above, where conflicts cannot be mitigated to an acceptable degree, they are typically disclosed or in extreme circumstances Alamut may decline to act for the client in question. Conflicts are scored for both their gross or inherent impact and subsequently their net or residual impact once the Firm’s mitigating controls have been taken into account. The gross scores are Alamut’s generic assessment of the conflict in question as it affects the industry, and these do not take into account the Firm’s business model or control framework. As a result, there can be significant differences between the gross and net scores. The scores awarded are High, Medium High, Medium Low and Low. Some conflicts arise constantly and are inherent in the business activities of the Firm whereas others only arise as a result of discretionary actions of individuals. The scoring process is to a degree subjective but the consideration of individual conflicts includes the likelihood of the conflict materialising as well as the impact that it would have were it to occur. The regulatory obligations and requirements in relation to individual conflicts is also considered, in particular at the gross level. Conflicts will likely be scored as High at a gross level for example where they relate to possible market abuse. We consider the effect of the mitigating controls and determine the materiality of their impact upon the gross score, including the likelihood of the conflict impacting a client.

The scoring of the conflicts will be reviewed periodically by the Governing Body, typically annually or more frequently if there is a material change.

policies.content:
Cybersecurity Policy

Cybersecurity Policy Introduction

The Financial Conduct Authority ("FCA") has emphasised the importance of cybersecurity to the integrity of the market system, investor data protection and the wellbeing of the regulated firm. This Cybersecurity Policy sets out the policies and procedures of Alamut in respect of the operations of its business activities. Alamut strives to foster a security culture within the Firm, driven from the top down - from the Board down to every employee. In addition, this policy serves to provide to Alamut’s Board, senior management and members of staff (herein, “employees” or “you”) with an awareness and understanding of the applicable cybersecurity requirements and best practices, to enable them and Alamut to effectively comply with industry standards and regulatory requirements. To ensure that Alamut can maintain business as usual in the face of disruption, the Firm also maintains a separate Business Continuity Plan (“BCP”). The BCP addresses mitigation of the effects of a cybersecurity incident and/or recovery from such an incident.

Background to Alamut and Regulatory Permissions

Alamut an investment management firm based in London (UK) and is authorised and regulated by the FCA as a full scope AIFM and Collective Portfolio Management Investment (‘CMPI’) Firm. Alamut seeks to generate long-term growth of capital and income through full investment in the ordinary shares, primarily in equity and equity related securities and instruments, in the Master Fund. The Master Fund primarily invests in, or takes exposure to, liquid and tradable equities that the Investment Manager considers structurally advantaged companies with economic tailwinds and strong management. Long idea generation is a fundamental bottom-up process and deep due diligence to drive conviction, which intrinsically drives a 3-5 year horizon. The typical holding period of a long position for the Firm is 1.5-2 years.

Additionally, the Master Fund employs an active short strategy, which seeks to sell (short) over-valued securities where the market is, and the companies themselves are, structurally over-optimistic. Short ideas are driven by proprietary forensic accounting screens, proprietary data capture and insights from fundamental research. The typical holding period of a short position for the Firm is 3-12 months.

What is Cybersecurity?

Cybersecurity consists of technologies, processes and measures that are designed to protect systems, networks and data from cyber-crimes. Effective cybersecurity reduces the risk of a cyber-attack and protects entities, organisations and individuals from the deliberate exploitation of systems, networks and technologies.

Regulatory background

The FCA has stated that cyber risks pose a threat to financial services firms and as such firms should be:       

Aware of the threat Able to defend themselves effectively Able to respond proportionately to cyber events Develop a “security culture” throughout the organization (i.e. from the Governing Body down to every employee) Able to identify and prioritise their information assets (i.e. hardware, software, people) Able to protect these assets, detect breaches, respond to, and recover from incidents Able to constantly evolve to meet new threats

Roles and responsibilities

In light of Alamut’s business activities and simple firm and team structure, the Firm does not engage an external IT service provider.

The Chief Operating Officer is the main person responsible for IT systems and controls at the Firm- supported by the Operations Manager. Refer to Appendix A for the schedule of roles and responsibilities. The COO will review and update this policy at least annually, and conduct periodic reviews of the Firm’s cybersecurity preparedness as set forth in this policy. Whenever an employee has a question regarding the requirements of the policy, the employee should discuss with the COO.

Training and Awareness Just like for compliance matters, the culture of Alamut will determine how Cybersecurity is dealt with. Alamut has instituted Cybersecurity training and awareness programs through its RFA, KnowBe4 & Drawbridge. The Firm's external compliance consultant also covers cybersecurity in the annual compliance training. This training is all computer based and is there to encourages all staff at Alamut to 'think before you click' on either their work, home, mobile, or other device. A lot of it is common sense, but it still needs to become part of the individuals’ mindset.

Alamut’s Information and Cybersecurity Policy Information Security It is the Firm's policy to employ the following practices on a periodic basis for the management of information security assets within the Firm. Assets generally include hardware (e.g. servers and switches), software (e.g. mission critical applications and support systems) and confidential information. Assets should be protected from illicit access, use, disclosure, alteration, destruction, and/or theft, resulting in loss to the organisation. On an ongoing basis:   

Prioritise the resources (hardware, data, and software) for protection based on their sensitivity and business value, as they are added to the network, as well as annually (internal process); Implement changes to processes where necessary to meet with industry best practices. Catalogue connections to Alamut's network from external sources, investigate alerts promptly, and further examine logs.

At least quarterly:  

Maintain and update maps of network resources, connections, and data flows (including locations where investor data is housed); and Assess logging in capabilities and practices for adequacy and security requiring a minimum level of complexity for user access passwords.

At least annually:   

Perform inventory reviews of physical devices and systems; Perform inventory review of software platforms and applications; and If felt applicable conduct third party audits in order to test the process, including obtaining the SOC2 report from relevant counterparties (SOC2 focuses on a business’s non-financial reporting controls as they relate to security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy of a system).

Identification of Cybersecurity Risks and Governance

The Firm conducts annual risk assessments to (i) identify cybersecurity threats, vulnerabilities, and potential business consequences and to (ii) identify physical security threats and vulnerabilities that may have an impact on cybersecurity. Documentation of such assessments include:    

The business group or individual who conducted the assessment; Date of completion of the assessment; Any findings from the assessment that were deemed to be moderate or high risk; and Details of how the risks were remedied.

Alamut also maintains insurance that specifically covers losses and expenses attributable to cybersecurity incidents, including a description of the nature of the coverage, and in the event of any claims filed. The Firm shall document the nature of the resolution of such claims.

Protection of Firm Networks and Information

The Firm utilises the following practices and controls regarding the protection of its networks and information:           

Provides written guidance and periodic training to all staff concerning information security risks and responsibilities. Maintains controls to prevent unauthorised escalation of user privileges and lateral movement among network resources. Restricts users to those network resources necessary for their business functions. Maintains an environment for testing and development of software and applications that is separate from its business environment. Maintains a baseline configuration of hardware and software, and users are prevented from altering that environment without authorisation (all hardware is configured to a pre-defined standard and any changes are required to be approved by the COO). Maintains a process for the removal, transfers, and disposition of IT assets. Has a process in place for ensuring regular system maintenance, including timely installation of software patches that address security vulnerabilities. Maintains controls to secure removable and portable media against malware and data leakage, and locks down end points to prevent any removable media from being used. Maintains protection against Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks for critical internet-facing IP addresses. Alamut maintains a written Cybersecurity Incident Response Policy (see Appendix B) and conducts tests or exercises to assess its Cybersecurity Incident Response Policy. The firm periodically tests the functionality of its backup system for the Firm.

Risks Associated with Remote Access and WFH

Alamut has allowed for a hybrid working structure, which adds another layer of risk to the risks that the Firm faces
as they require access to the Firm’s systems remotley. This has been mitigated somewhat by two factors, as follows:
1) Issuing employees with company opened and maintained equipment that is regularly monitored and
patched; and
2) Introducing a s secure cloud based environment.

Risks Associated with Vendors and Other Third Parties

Alamut outsources certain administrative functions to third party services providers (e.g., accountants, lawyers, etc.). To mitigate risks associated with the use of third party service providers, none have access to Alamut's IT systems, but some may have access to the Firm's data. The Firm:    

Conducts or requires cybersecurity risk assessments of vendors and business partners with access to the Alamut’s networks, investor data, or other sensitive information, or due to the cybersecurity risk of the outsourced function. Incorporates requirements relating to cybersecurity risk into its contracts with vendors and business partners. Ensures that vendors and business partners received training related to information security procedures and responsibilities (if they can access Alamut's network). Periodically assesses the segregation of sensitive network resources from resources accessible by third parties.

Detection of Unauthorised Activity

Alamut employs the following practices to assist in detecting unauthorised activity on its networks and devices. However all employees at the Firm are responsible for detecting and reporting suspected unauthorised activity to the COO:       

Informs the Firm about expected events on the Firm’s network. Monitors the Firm’s network environment to detect potential cybersecurity events. Monitors the activity of third party service providers with access to the Firm’s networks. Monitor for the presence of unauthorised users, devices, connections, and software on the Firm’s networks. Use data loss prevention software. Conduct penetration tests and vulnerability scans, and this is documented (i.e. the Firm maintains documentation of the findings from the test and/or assessment, that are deemed to be potentially moderate or high risk and information on how these were addressed). The analysis of events is utilised to improve Alamut’s defensive measures and policies.

Cyber Security Situations

Although Alamut believes it is a low-risk when it comes to a cybersecurity attack (due to its low-profile, low volume of data-traffic and secure network) it has considered some potential scenarios which could occur, along with the required response, which are described in the table below. Potential incidents will be added to this list as and when they become popular, unfortunately it is a fast-growing area of crime.

In the event that Alamut experiences a cyber-attack, it will report the incident to the appropriate regulatory authorities and/or the police, as applicable. In addition, the Firm will fully document the incident (i.e. effects on the Firm, or its vendors, or other relevant third parties; the extent to which losses were incurred, investor information accessed, and Firm services impacted, the date of the incident, the date the incident was discovered and the remediation for such incident).

Potential Incidents DDOS (Distributed Denial of Service) Attack

Trojans



By following the appropriate Firm Internet and email  usage requirements many of these types of threats will be countered. The comprehensive and up-todate virus definitions running on all of the Firm's PCs, should ensure that Trojans are stopped at source.

Phishing/Spear phishing Attacks

The user/employee must immediately physically disconnect the PC from the network and inform the COO without delay. RFA will then attempt to remove the threat. If needed, specialised third parties will be contracted to assist. The PC will not be allowed back onto the network until the threat has been removed (the employee may use an alternative computer to work in the meantime).

If you notice any signs of a phishing attack, do not open the e-mail or link, etc. In an instance where you open a phishing email, you should follow the same procedure as for a Trojan (see above). You will receive training on the subject to ensure it does not reoccur. Immediately physically disconnect the PC from the network and inform the COO or RFA without delay.

Always check to see if the email has really come from the person it says it has.

Criminals may deliver different types of phishing attacks such as links, attachments, fake websites requesting usernames/passwords, and requests to download rogue applications.

These types of attacks are commonly effective when the employee is at fault.

Network Worms

 

These can be defeated by regular patching, disabling

auto-run capabilities, and strong password policies.  Many network worms (e.g. Conficker) will try to exploit network shares by logging on using a list of



built-in, bad passwords (e.g. 12345, password 1, password2). The network firewall is also in place to

You must always follow the Firm’s strict password policy that has been put in place. Do not write down your ID and password (always memorise it). Do not share your password with other users/employees (no matter how senior). If you become aware of a network worm incident, immediately physically disconnect the PC from the network and inform RFA or the COO without delay.

Ransomware



See “phishing e-mail” above.

The FCA stated that they expect the ransomware threat to firms and customers to significantly increase

over the next few years. These attacks often involve the use of a phishing email, which once clicked on installs the ransomware onto a system, encrypts key information within it (effectively locking users out of systems or preventing access to data), and the hackers demand a ransom to release it. Ransomware attackers are very sophisticated.

Direct-Access Attack

 

Physically gaining entry to the systems, network and

documents. The Firm’s office building is protected by  a keycard entry door; CCTV are in operation and a security officer monitors all individuals entering and exiting the building 24/7. To gain access to Alamut's offices, one would need to be in possession of a staff pass.

  

You must keep your staff pass safe at all times; if you lose it, report to the COO without delay. Lock your PC whenever you are away from your desk. Clear all confidential information from your desk each evening. Use the confidential waste disposal bin to dispose of sensitive documents. If this attack occurs, immediately inform the COO. The COO will, as appropriate, involve the police.

Miscellaneous

For employees’ information, the general term “malware”, short for malicious software, is an umbrella term used to refer to a variety of forms of hostile or intrusive software, including computer viruses, worms, Trojan horses, ransomware, spyware, adware, scareware, and other malicious programs. It can take the form of executable code, scripts, active content, and other software. Malware is defined by its malicious intent, acting against the requirements of the computer user, and so does not include software that causes unintentional harm due to some deficiency. Software such as anti-virus and firewalls are used to protect against activity identified as malicious, and to recover from attacks. Examples of consequences of a cyber-attack:    

The intruder could take control of a user's PC and do anything they want based on the permissions they have. This could involve the stealing/deletion of data, obtaining passwords, creating false documents, or instructions, etc,. The availability of a critical Alamut web or network resource is impaired by a software or hardware malfunction. Alamut's network is breached by an unauthorised user. The compromise of an external third-party computer used to remotely access the Firm’s network resulted in fraudulent activity, such as efforts to fraudulently transfer funds, or the submission of fraudulent payment requests purportedly on behalf of a vendor. The Firm is subject of an extortion attempt by an individual or group threatening to impair access to or damage to Alamut’s data, devices, network, or web services. An employee or other authorised user of the Firm’s network engaged in misconduct resulting in the misappropriation of funds, securities, sensitive investor or Firm information, or damage to the Firm's network or data.

Reporting a cyber incident

Under the FCA’s Principle 11, Alamut must report material cyber incidents to the FCA. See Appendix B for the Firm’s Cybersecurity Incident Response Policy. An incident may be considered a material incident if it:   

Results in significant loss of data, or the availability or control of the Firm’s IT systems. Impacts a large number of victims. Results in unauthorised access to, or malicious software present on, your information and communication systems.

Appendix B – Cybersecurity Incident Response Policy

This appendix provides some policy guidelines and procedures for handling cybersecurity incidents. The term “cybersecurity incident” is defined as any irregular or adverse event that threatens the security, integrity, or availability of the information resources on any part of the Firm network. Some examples of security incidents are:    

Illegal access of a company computer system (e.g. a hacker logs onto a server and copies the password file). Damage to a Firm computer system or network caused by illegal access (e.g. a cyber-attack releasing a virus or worm). Denial of service attack against a Firm web server (e.g. a hacker initiates a flood of packets against a web server designed to cause the system to crash). Malicious use of system resources to launch an attack against other computer outside of the Firm network (e.g. the system administrator notices a connection to an unknown network and a strange process accumulating a lot of server time).

Employees, who believe their terminal or computer systems have been subjected to a cybersecurity incident, or has otherwise been improperly accessed or used, should report the situation to the CCO immediately. The employee must immediately turn off the computer, directly from the wall plug if possible. Leaving the computer in the condition it was in when the security incident was discovered will assist in identifying the source of the problem and in determining the steps that should be taken to remedy the problem. How to report a cyber-incident If Alamut judges an incident to be material, the Firm will report it as follows: 

By emailing the FCA at firm.queries@fca.org.uk



By calling the FCA on 0300 500 0597 (available Monday to Friday during office hours).



If Alamut is suffering a live cyber-attack that is in progress, the Firm will call Action Fraud on 0300 123 2040 (the Firm will not report by using Action Fraud’s online tool service, as this may cause unnecessary delay). Website at: https://www.actionfraud.police.uk/reporting-fraud-and-cyber-crime



If the incident is a data breach, Alamut will also report the incident to the Information Commissioner's Office on 0303 123 1113 (available Monday to Friday during office hours). The Firm can also utilise the online report, a breach forms (see Appendix C for links). Website at: https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/report-a-breach/

policies.summary: Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Policy Introduction The purpose of this Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Policy (the "Policy") is to ensure that Alamut Investment Management LLP can effectively respond to disruptions, safeguard investor assets, comply with regu
policies.content:
Disaster Recovery & Business Continuity Policy

Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Policy Introduction The purpose of this Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Policy (the "Policy") is to ensure that Alamut Investment Management LLP can effectively respond to disruptions, safeguard investor assets, comply with regulatory obligations, and maintain operations during and after a disaster or unexpected event.

Scope This Policy applies to all employees, contractors, vendors, systems, and processes associated with Alamut Investment Management LLP. It encompasses technology infrastructure, critical business operations, and investor communications.

Policy Statement Alamut Investment Management LLP is committed to: Protecting investor assets and sensitive information. Ensuring continuity of operations under adverse circumstances. Complying with applicable regulations and industry best practices. Regularly testing and improving disaster recovery and business continuity capabilities.

Roles and Responsibilities Executive Management: Oversee implementation and ensure adequate resource allocation for disaster recovery and business continuity planning. Disaster Recovery Team (DRT): Coordinate technical recovery efforts for IT systems and data. COO and Compliance & Operations Manager

Business Continuity Team (BCT): Ensure continuity of critical business processes, investor communications, and compliance functions. COO, Head of Operations and Office Manager/Executive Assistant Employees: Adhere to the procedures outlined in this Policy and participate in training and testing activities.

Risk Assessment Alamut Investment Management LLP conducts regular risk assessments to identify and evaluate potential threats, including: Cyberattacks Natural disasters System failures Vendor disruptions Pandemics or health crises

Key Objectives Recovery Time Objective (RTO): Restore critical systems within 1 hour. Recovery Point Objective (RPO): Ensure no more than 1 hour of data loss. Business Continuity Goal: Resume priority operations (e.g., trading, compliance, investor communication) within 1 hour.

Disaster Recovery Procedures Data Backup and Restoration: Data backups are performed daily and stored in secure, geographically diverse locations.

Restoration procedures are tested [quarterly/annually]. System Redundancy: Critical systems are mirrored on redundant servers or cloud-based platforms. Cybersecurity Incident Response: Activate the Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan in case of a breach. Isolate affected systems and notify the Disaster Recovery Team immediately. Testing and Validation: Conduct full DR simulations at least once per year. Document and address any deficiencies.

Business Continuity Procedures Critical Functions Prioritization: Trading, compliance, and investor communications are designated as Tier 1 priorities. Manual workarounds are documented for essential functions. Remote Work Protocols: Employees are equipped with remote access tools (e.g., secure VPN, cloud platforms). A test of remote capabilities is conducted semi-annually. Vendor Management: All critical third-party vendors must maintain robust DR and BCP capabilities. Annual reviews of vendor agreements and plans are required.

Communication Plan Internal Communication: Notify employees of emergencies via [email/SMS/mass notification system]. Maintain a current employee contact list and escalation protocols. External Communication: Pre-approved templates for investor and regulator notifications will be used. Designate [name/position] as the primary spokesperson for external communications.

Compliance and Oversight Regulatory Requirements: Adhere to SEC Rule 206(4)-7, FCA SYSC guidelines, and other applicable regulations. Documentation: Maintain records of all DR and BCP activities for at least [X] years. Submit reports to stakeholders as required. Audits and Updates: The Policy is reviewed annually and after significant business or technological changes. Results of audits and testing are reported to the Executive Management.

Enforcement Failure to adhere to this Policy may result in disciplinary action, including termination, as outlined in the Alamut Investment Management LLP Employee Handbook.

Effective Date and Review This Policy is effective as of 1st Jan 2025 and will be reviewed and updated annually or as needed.

Approval
Approved by: ExCo
30 Dec 2024

This policy ensures the resilience and operational continuity of Alamut Investment Management LLP, safeguarding assets and trust during challenging circumstances.

policies.summary: Introduction Alamut Investment Management LLP (“Alamut” or the “Firm”) personnel may find it helpful to interview an expert consultant or use an expert consulting service to identify and arrange an interview with an expert consultant. The following are policies and procedures wit
policies.content:
Expert Networks Policy

Introduction Alamut Investment Management LLP (“Alamut” or the “Firm”) personnel may find it helpful to interview an expert consultant or use an expert consulting service to identify and arrange an interview with an expert consultant. The following are policies and procedures with respect to the Firm’s use of expert consulting firms and expert consultants. Where possible, you should use one of Alamut approved expert networks on the basis that they are:

(i)

The market leaders;

(ii)

They have the most appropriate network of experts for the Firm’s investment strategy;

(iii)

We are familiar with how they operate; and

(iv)

We are satisfied with their internal compliance procedures and controls.

The approved list of expert networks is detailed in Appendix A.

Regarding all such consultations, employees must be careful to ensure that information received through these consultations is not material non-public information. To the extent an interview with an expert consultant raises a concern about possible exposure to material non-public information, you should immediately contact the Compliance Officer (“CCO”).

Should you wish to use an expert network other than those listed in Appendix A or contact an expert directly, specific approval from the CCO must be obtained following the process below. This also applies to any internal introductions or sourced contacts made by internal primary research personnel.

For the avoidance of doubt, these policies relate only to the use of consultants who are being paid by the Firm or its client, the Alamut Master Fund, for their advice.

Registered Oﬃce: 175 Piccadilly, London, W1J 9EN, United Kingdom

Compliance Approval

Using an expert network for the first time If you would like to use an expert network which has not previously been approved by compliance, you must obtain written approval from the CCO. Alamut must conduct a screening interview to ensure that the Expert Consulting Firm has policies and procedures: Regarding the hiring of expert consultants; To train and educate expert consultants on issues surrounding the dissemination of material non-public information; To notify Alamut’s CCO of all consultant requests and meetings; To flag to the CCO where an individual consultant is contacted more than four times in a rolling 12month period; To ensure that no consultants are engaged who have been employed by a public limited company which is the subject of the Alamut analyst’s research leading to the use of the consultant in the last 6 months; To determine whether the expert consultant is subject to any non-disclosure agreement or otherwise prohibited from discussing information relating to any industry, company or security. Upon approval, the CCO will send an e-mail to all employees confirming that a new expert network has been approved for use. A list of such networks is detailed in Appendix A.

Using expert consultants outside of an expert network While it is strongly encouraged that you use the Firm’s expert networks (as set out in Appendix A) where possible, there may be occasions where this is not possible or appropriate. The Firm may use sourced contacts made by internal primary research personnel. If an internal primary research person identifies and wishes to use a contact or expert directly, the following process must be adhered to: Written CCO approval is required before engaging with the sourced contact. The CCO will review for compliance and conflict-of-interest concerns. If approved, the CCO will provide written confirmation with guidelines. In addition to the above, refer to the section below on consultants not from the list of approved expert networks.

Calls with Expert Consultants

Scheduling a call with a consultant not from the list of approved expert networks Prior to scheduling a call with an expert consultant, you must seek pre-approval from the CCO. Requests for approval should include: The name of the expert consulting firm (where applicable); Whether the expert is or has worked at a listed company in the last 12 months; and The intended type of information being sought (including the industries or companies of interest). Note: Alamut analysts cannot speak to consultants who have been employed by target investment either as a direct employee or as a consultant in the last six months. All calls and follow up calls, where possible, should be conducted through an expert network. This provides the firm with an extra layer of protection should material non-public information be passed across to a Firm’s employee. If approved, compliance will provide you with written authorisation to arrange a call with the expert consultant. All calls must be set up and conducted from a landline in the office. A conference call should be set up and a calendar invite with dial in details sent to the CCO. The calendar invite SUBJECT should have the expert consultant’s name. The calendar invite BODY should have the topic (a short description) and a bio, along with the dial in details. Optima Partners and the CCO will randomly dial into the meetings. The CCO should be informed if you intend to schedule a fourth meeting with the same consultant in any rolling 12-month period. Once approval is granted, documentation of the approval and any subsequent interactions with the sourced contact must be maintained. Internal primary research personnel are responsible for reporting these interactions to the CCO in accordance with the firm’s reporting procedures.

Standard Script A standard script must be read at the start of every call with an expert consultant who has not been introduced via an approved expert network: Initial call: “Hello, I am X. I work at Alamut Investment Management LLP. I would like to remind you not to share any material non-public information with us and to please honour any confidentiality agreements you have in place.” Follow up call: “Hello, it is X from Alamut Investment Management LLP. I must remind you not to share any material non-public information with us and to please honour any confidentiality agreements you have in place.”

Material Non-Public Information

Material Non-Public Information (MNPI) refers to confidential information that, if made public, could have a significant impact on the market price or value of a company's securities or other financial instruments. Material Information:

This is any information that a reasonable investor would consider important when making a decision to buy, sell, or hold securities. It is information that could potentially influence the price of those securities or an investor's decision to trade.

Non-Public Information: This is information that has not been disseminated to the general public or widely shared in a manner that would make it available to investors or market participants. Examples of MNPI might include: Earnings results that have not yet been released to the public. Major changes in corporate strategy, such as mergers, acquisitions, or sales. Key management changes, such as the appointment or departure of CEOs. Significant product developments or regulatory approvals. FCA and Insider Trading The FCA regulates and enforces rules to prevent the misuse of MNPI, particularly in the context of insider trading. According to the Market Abuse Regulation (MAR), it is illegal for individuals to: Trade based on MNPI. Disclose MNPI to others unless it’s within the course of normal duties. Recommend or encourage others to trade based on MNPI. Using MNPI for personal gain or to manipulate the market is a serious offense under the FCA’s regulatory framework, and penalties can include heavy fines and imprisonment. Procedure if material non-public information has been shared If you have any concerns that during a call with an expert consultant material non-public information may have been shared with you or there is a question raised about whether material non-public information has been shared, you must immediately inform the CCO. The information should not be shared with any person other than the CCO. To the extent the CCO concludes or has reason to believe that material non-public information has been disclosed by an expert consultant, or that an expert consultant has shared information with Alamut, in violation of a duty of confidentiality, the CCO must notify the expert consulting service that referred the expert consultant (if applicable). The CCO must also consider whether any further action should be taken. It is essential that Alamut never trades or attempts to trade on material non-public information as the consequences could result in significant fines for the firm, the individual that had the information could be subject to criminal and civil liability and unquantifiable reputational damage. Any violation of the firm’s policy

on the use of material non-public information see ‘Civil Offences - Market Abuse’ of the Compliance Manual) may result in immediate dismissal and the relevant authorities being informed. Calls with expert consultants from listed companies If you speak with an expert consultant, who is either currently working or has worked at a listed company or a listed company’s subsidiary / affiliate within the last 12 months, you must be particularly careful that no material non-public information is shared with you. There will be an automatic presumption that such expert’s consultants are in the possession of material non-public information. Receipt of such information may prevent Alamut from trading a specific stock and could result in a regulatory full investigation. It is therefore very important that you do not discuss: Any details concerning the listed company or its group / affiliates, where he has worked in the last 12 months; Any deals or transactions related to that entity; and Any market rumours. You may discuss: General industry trends; Other unrelated entities; and Historic / public information. These are not complete lists but are there to provide you with a steer on the conversations you may have with expert consultants at listed companies.

Compliance Monitoring

Call Monitoring The CCO will randomly dial into calls with expert consultants. You will not be informed when this is occurring and a report will be maintained detailing the call. The CCO will monitor and maintain a spreadsheet of all calls where pre-approval has been requested, when and if the call took place and whether employees have sent the standard e-mail within 48 hours.

Trade Monitoring The CCO monitors trades to see if any significant movements in the price of a stock occur following a consultation with an expert consultant. A restricted list and an insider list will be maintained if we receive any inside information. This will be checked against trades placed by the fund(s).

Appendix A

Approved Expert Networks

AlphaSights Ltd Gerson Lehrman Group Guidepoint Third Bridge

policies.title: IT Security Policy
policies.summary: IT Security Policy Purpose: To establish robust IT security measures that ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data, protect client assets, prevent financial crime, and ensure compliance with FCA regulations, GDPR, and other applicable laws.
policies.content:
IT Security Policy

IT Security Policy Purpose: To establish robust IT security measures that ensure the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data, protect client assets, prevent financial crime, and ensure compliance with FCA regulations, GDPR, and other applicable laws.

Scope: This policy applies to all employees, contractors, third parties, and systems used by the company to store, process, or transmit data.

Governance and Accountability SM&CR Responsibilities: Assign a Senior Manager (e.g., Chief Information Security Officer, CISO) with ultimate responsibility for IT security. Ensure periodic reporting on IT security risks to the Board and senior management. Policies and Procedures: Regularly review and update IT security policies to ensure compliance with FCA, GDPR, and industry standards. Document and maintain a Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP).

Risk Management Conduct regular cybersecurity risk assessments to identify, mitigate, and monitor IT risks, as required under SYSC 3.2.19R. Implement a third-party risk management framework to assess vendors and outsourcing arrangements. Include IT security in the company’s ICAAP (Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process), if applicable.

Data Security Confidentiality: Encrypt sensitive client data (at rest and in transit) using industry standards (e.g., AES-256).

Implement role-based access controls (RBAC) to restrict access to sensitive data on a "need-to-know" basis. Integrity: Use checksums and hashing (e.g., SHA-256) to verify data integrity. Maintain an audit trail for changes to critical data and systems. Availability: Ensure uptime through redundant systems, backups, and robust disaster recovery measures. Conduct periodic recovery testing for key systems.

Network and Infrastructure Security Implement a firewall and intrusion detection/prevention system (IDS/IPS) to monitor and filter traffic. Use a VPN for secure remote access to company systems. Segment networks to isolate critical systems and sensitive data. Regularly patch and update all systems and software to mitigate vulnerabilities.

Endpoint and Device Security Enforce endpoint protection (antivirus, anti-malware, and EDR solutions) across all devices. Implement a Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution to secure mobile and remote devices. Use encryption and password policies for laptops, tablets, and smartphones.

Access Controls Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) for accessing systems, especially for privileged accounts. Use strong password policies, including minimum length, complexity, and periodic changes. Maintain a centralized identity and access management (IAM) system.

Incident Response and Reporting Maintain an Incident Response Plan (IRP) to address potential cyberattacks, data breaches, or system failures. Define reporting procedures to comply with FCA’s Principle 11 for notifying material cyber incidents promptly. Conduct root cause analysis (RCA) for all incidents and implement lessons learned.

Training and Awareness Provide mandatory cybersecurity training for all employees, including phishing awareness and secure handling of data. Conduct regular simulated phishing tests to measure awareness. Include IT security responsibilities in employee contracts and provide regular policy updates.

Monitoring and Auditing Continuously monitor systems for anomalies or breaches using SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) tools. Conduct periodic penetration testing and vulnerability assessments. Perform regular internal and external IT audits to ensure compliance with FCA and GDPR requirements.

Outsourcing and Third-Party Risk Management Conduct due diligence on all third-party vendors and service providers to ensure they meet FCA cybersecurity requirements. Require third-party providers to adhere to contractual Service Level Agreements (SLAs) with defined IT security standards. Periodically assess vendors’ compliance with security policies and conduct audits where necessary.

Data Protection and GDPR Compliance Implement measures to ensure compliance with GDPR requirements, including secure data storage, processing, and subject rights requests. Maintain a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) process for new IT systems or significant changes. Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) if required.

Operational Resilience Design systems with resilience in mind to minimize downtime in case of cyberattacks or technical failures, as per FCA’s Operational Resilience Policy. Regularly test the Business Continuity Plan (BCP) and Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP). Implement backup solutions and test recovery scenarios at least annually.

Reporting and Record-Keeping Maintain logs of all IT security events for at least 6 years, as required by FCA SYSC 9.1.1R. Report material cyber incidents to the FCA under Principle 11 and notify the ICO (Information Commissioner’s Office) within 72 hours for GDPR-reportable breaches.

Review and Updates Review this policy annually or in response to significant regulatory, technological, or business changes. Conduct periodic testing of IT security measures, including cyber resilience exercises, to ensure effectiveness.

Summary This IT Security Policy ensures the company's compliance with FCA requirements while safeguarding client data and maintaining operational resilience. It should be regularly updated and adapted to reflect evolving cybersecurity threats, regulatory changes, and business needs.

policies.title: Order Execution Policy
policies.summary: Alamut Investment Management LLP (‘Alamut’) is an investment manager providing portfolio management services, and reception and transmission services, for its clients. For the purpose of this policy, the Firm’s ‘clients’ are any alternative investment funds (‘AIF’ or ‘fund’) the
policies.content:
Order Execution Policy

Order Execution Policy Introduction

Alamut Investment Management LLP (‘Alamut’) is an investment manager providing portfolio management services, and reception and transmission services, for its clients. For the purpose of this policy, the Firm’s ‘clients’ are any alternative investment funds (‘AIF’ or ‘fund’) the firm manages as an AIFM and any separately managed accounts / segregated portfolio mandates (‘SMA’) the firm provides its asset management services to. The Firm may either directly execute transactions in financial instruments directly in the market or, alternatively, places order with other entities for execution (‘execution entities’). Both the Firm or the execution entities may execute some orders outside of a trading venue (being a regulated market or a multilateral trading facility or an organised trading facility) which may result in counterparty risk. Additional information about the consequences of this means of execution will be provided to clients upon request. The Firm recognises its duty to act in the best interests of our clients when either executing or placing or transmitting such orders for execution. The Firm has ensured that any entity it transmits orders to, has classified the firm as a ‘Professional Client’ and therefore owes the firm a duty of best execution.

Meaning of ‘best interests of our clients’ The Firm is required to take all “sufficient steps” to obtain the best possible result for our clients, considering the following ‘execution factors’: •

Price Costs

•

Speed

•

Likelihood of execution and settlement

•

Size

•

Nature or any other consideration relevant to the execution of an order

When determining the relative importance of these execution factors the following ‘execution criteria’ must be taken into account: •

The characteristics of the client including the categorisation of the client as retail or professional

•

The characteristics of the client order, including where the order involves a securities financing transaction

•

The characteristics of financial instruments that are the subject of that order

•

The characteristics of the execution venues to which that order can be directed

It should be noted that the Firm will satisfy its obligation to take all sufficient steps to obtain the best possible result for a client to the extent that the firm executes an order or a specific aspect of an order following specific instructions from a client relating to that order or the specific aspect of that order, notwithstanding the fact that following such instructions may prevent the firm from obtaining the best possible result. In the case of OTC products, including bespoke products, the Firm will check the fairness of the price proposed to the client, by gathering market data used in the estimation of the price of such products and, where possible, by comparing with similar or comparable products.

Prioritisation of execution factors Outside of any specific instructions provided by a client, the Firm considers the following to be the most important execution factors in order of prioritisation: •

Price

•

Likelihood of execution

•

Size

•

Costs

•

Speed

•

Other considerations

The prioritisation of execution factors is a standing agenda item on the firm’s monthly Investment Committee.

Disclosure

The Firm does not receive any third-party payments or non-monetary benefits for routing orders or from the trading venues so selected for the execution of orders (or, where relevant, from the execution venues selected) and nor does it impose any charges upon any other participants in a transaction. To the extent that any nonmonetary benefits are received the firm will ensure that: •

any such payments or non-monetary benefits will not impair the firm’s requirement to act in the best interests of its clients

•

any such payments or non-monetary benefits will conform to the regulatory requirements concerning ‘conflicts of interest’ and ‘inducements

•

clients are informed of such payments and non-monetary benefits

Further information about the Firm’s policies or arrangements and how they are reviewed will be provided upon request.

Client Consent

It is a requirement that the Firm obtains the prior consent of its clients to the Firm’s order execution policy. Provision of a copy of this policy to a client, and in the absence of any comment, will be deemed to satisfy the need for prior consent. The execution of a client order may on occasion take place outside of a trading venue, being a regulated market, a multilateral trading facility or an organised trading facility. The firm is required to obtain the express prior consent of its clients before proceeding to execute orders outside of a trading venue. Orders executed outside a trading venue may give rise to counterparty risk. Additional information about the consequences of this means of execution will be provided on request.

Effectiveness

The effectiveness of this policy in delivering the best possible result for clients will be regularly monitored by the firm to enable identification, and rectification, of any deficiencies. As part of the daily review of trades undertaken, the Chief Investment Officer (“CIO”) will also look for ‘outlier trades’ where the price appears inconsistent with similar trades. A more formal, and independent, review is undertaken by the Compliance Officer on a monthly basis in which a random selection of trades is identified and reviewed for ‘best execution’. The Compliance Officer will liaise with the relevant members within the firm’s trading team as necessary as part of this review. Apart from the Compliance Officer’s normal reporting lines, any inconsistencies identified from the review of sample trades will be reported to the CIO for review and comment to the compliance function, and consideration of any necessary escalation. This policy and the arrangements in place will be reviewed: a)

whenever the firm assesses that a material change has occurred which would affect the firm’s ability to continue to obtain the best possible result for clients. A ‘material change’ would include a significant event that impacted upon the execution factors or the execution criteria (see above); and

b)

at least annually

Execution venues used in compliance with the firm’s obligations when it executes transactions

Outside of any specific instructions provided by a client, the firm has identified executions venues for the execution of trades in order to ensure the best possible result for the client on a consistent basis. The venues are selected by the Investment Committee and are subject to ongoing review (see ‘Effectiveness’ above).
