policies.title: Privacy Notice
policies.summary: Privacy Notice Alamut Investment Management LLP is a company registered in England and Wales with company number OC451305. Alamut Investment Management LLP (“The Firm” or “Alamut”) is authorised and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCA”) with Firm Reference Number 1
policies.content:
Privacy Notice

Privacy Notice Alamut Investment Management LLP is a company registered in England and Wales with company number OC451305. Alamut Investment Management LLP (“The Firm” or “Alamut”) is authorised and regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (“FCA”) with Firm Reference Number 1015941. For the purposes of the General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’), Alamut will be the ‘controller’ of the personal data you provide. Please read the following information carefully in order to understand the Firm’s practices in relation to the treatment of your personal data. Should you have any questions, please email us at compliance@alamut-im.com.

What data privacy principles does the Firm adhere to? 



The Firm will process all personal data in a lawfully, fair and transparent manner; 

The Firm will only collect personal data where it is necessary;



For the Firm to provide a service to you;



For you to provide a service to the Firm;



For the Firm to keep you informed of its products and services; or



For the Firm to comply with its legal and regulatory obligations.

The personal data collected by the Firm will be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the specific purpose for which your data will be processed;



The Firm will take all reasonable steps to ensure that personal data is accurate and, were necessary, kept up to date;



The Firm will maintain personal data in a form that permits identification no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data has been collected for processing, in accordance with the Firm’s record retention requirements as mandated by the Financial Conduct Authority;



The Firm will hold and process person data in a manner that ensures appropriate security;



The Firm will only share personal data where it is necessary to provide the agreed service or where it is necessary for the Firm to comply with its legal and regulatory requirements



The Firm will only utilise a service provider based outside of the UK for the processing of personal data where this is strictly necessary to facilitate our services to you. In all cases, we will ensure service providers are fully compliant with GDPR, or deemed equivalent, ahead of transferring any personal data.

What personal data does the Firm collect and why? While providing products/services to you, the Firm may collect information that is considered personal information (e.g. name, contact details, address, passport number, driving licence).

As a client, contact or employee of Alamut, we will require some personal information in order to verify your identity and have the applicable relationship with you. Some of this information may be required to satisfy legal obligations (e.g. to comply with obligations arising under the money laundering regulations whereas other information may be required in connection with the provision of services to you). The information collected will vary depending on the service the Firm provides to you, or you provide to the Firm, but typically includes: 

Personal information: Such as your name, date of birth, passport number or national insurance number;



Contact information: Including your address, telephone number and email address.

Where does the Firm store my personal data? The Firm has comprehensive policies and procedures in place to ensure your personal data is kept safe and secure, with these including: 

Data encryption;



Firewalls;



Intrusion detection;



24/7 physical protection of the facilities where your data is stored (i.e. Microsoft’s UK data centres);



Background checks for personnel that access physical facilities; and



Security procedures across all service operations.

How long does the Firm retain personal data? As a regulated entity, the Firm is required to maintain its books and records for a prescribed period (five years from either the ceasing of a business relationship, or, in the case of non-clients, from the making of a record – or alternatively, for seven years, where specifically requested to do so by the Financial Conduct Authority). As such, information that falls in scope of either of these requirements is retained in line with the mandated timeframe. Any information that is outside the scope of this requirement will be retained whilst relevant and useful, and destroyed where this ceases to be the case or where the data subject specifically requests this.

How have I been categorised in accordance with GDPR? The GDPR requires the Firm to inform you of the legal basis on which we maintain your personal data. Typically, the Firm will reach out to you personally to confirm this; however, as a rule the following is applicable: 

Clients – Information is maintained based on contractual obligation and/or legitimate interests (where relevant);



Service providers – Information is maintained based on contractual obligation; and



Database/marketing contacts – Information is maintained based on legitimate interest.

What are my rights? Once you have provided your details to the Firm, you have certain rights which apply, depending on your relationship with the Firm, the information you have shared with us and the Firm’s legal and regulatory obligations. 

You have the right to request a copy of the information that we hold about you. If you would like a copy of some, or all, of your personal information, please email the Firm at compliance@alamut-im.com. The Firm will provide this information to you within one month (with the ability to extend this by an additional two months where necessary), free of charge.



You have the right to request that the information the Firm holds about you is erased under certain circumstances including where there is no additional legal and/or regulatory requirement for the Firm to retain this information.



As a client, you have the right to request that any information the Firm holds about you be provided to another company in a commonly used and machine-readable format, otherwise known as ‘data portability’.



You have the right to ensure that your personal information is accurate and up to date, or where necessary rectified. Where you feel that your personal data is incorrect or inaccurate and should therefore be updated, please contact compliance@alamut-im.com



You have the right to object to your information being processed, for example for direct marketing purposes.



You have the right to restrict the processing of your information, for example limiting the material that you receive or where your information is transferred.



You have the right to object to any decisions based on the automated processing of your personal data, including profiling.



You have the right to lodge a complaint with the Information Commissioner’s Office https://ico.org.uk/concerns/ if you are not happy with the way that we manage or process personal data.

Will I be notified of changes to this policy? The Firm may, from time to time, review and update this policy. Compliance maintains the latest version of this policy and where the changes are deemed material, it will make you are aware of these.

Who should I direct questions to? If you have any questions, concerns or complaints about the practices contained within this document or how the Firm has handled your data, please email: compliance@alamut-im.com. Alternatively, you may write to: Alamut Investment Management LLP, 5-10 Bolton Street, London, W1J 8BA

policies.content:
Remuneration Policy

Remuneration Policy Regulatory context The Remuneration Policy (“Policy”) of Alamut Investment Management LLP (“Alamut”) is set out below, as required by SYSC 19B and in accordance with ESMA’s Guidelines on sound remuneration policies. The remuneration code under AIFMD is only applicable for the Firm’s first full performance period following authorisation as an AIFM.

Background to the Firm Alamut is authorised by the FCA as a Full-scope UK AIFM. As a Firm which is a collective portfolio management investment firm (“CPMI firm”), Alamut is subject to the Remuneration Code contained at SYSC 19B for the Firm’s AIFM business and SYSC 19G for the Firm’s non-AIFM business. For its non-MiFID business, Alamut is classified as a Small Non-Interconnected (“SNI”) firm and therefore only subject to the remuneration requirements within SYSC 19G which apply to SNI firms. The Firm will apply the requirements by applying SYSC 19B to all its staff.

Application The Firm has assessed the proportionality elements to determine whether it should disapply the Pay-out Process Rules. These rules are: a.

Retained units, shares or other instruments (SYSC 19B.1.17.R/Principle 5(e));

b.

Deferral (SYSC 19B.1.1.18.R/Principle 5(f)); and

c.

Performance adjustment (SYSC 19B.1.19.R and 19B.1.20.R/Principle 5(g))

The Firm has considered all the proportionality elements in line with the FCA Guidance, and this assessment is detailed in Appendix 1. Based on this assessment the Firm will disapply all the Pay-out Process Rules. In addition, there is no guaranteed variable remuneration and therefore the firm disapplies the Guaranteed Variable Remuneration Rule in SYSC 19B.1.14R (principle 5(b))

General Requirements Introduction UK AIFMs are required to have remuneration policies and practices in place which are consistent with, and promote, sound and effective risk management and do not encourage risk taking which is inconsistent with the risk profiles, rules or instruments traded by the AIF that they manage and of the AIFM itself. This remuneration policy is designed to encourage the alignment of the risks taken by the AIFM’s staff with those of the AIFs it manages, the investors of such AIFs and the AIFM itself; in particular, the policy takes into consideration the need to align risks in terms of risk management and exposure to risk. The policy is: •

In line with the business strategy, objectives, values and interests of the Firm;

•

Does not encourage excessive risk taking as compared to the investment policy of the AIFs the AIFM manages, and

•

Enables the Firm to align the interests of the AIFM and AIFs and their investors with those of the Firm’s staff subject to this policy as scheduled at Appendix 1 of this Policy (AIFM Remuneration Code Staff) that manage AIFs.

Gender neutral remuneration policies and practices The remuneration policy is gender neutral; this means that it is based on equality for male and female workers for equal work or work of equal value. The Firm strives to promote equality within the Firm with respect to employment, career development, promotion, and equal pay. Compliance with the Equality Act 2010 is integral to the remuneration policy and procedures. All remuneration decisions are based on the individual’s experience, performance and conduct.

AIFMD remuneration principles The Firm has determined the Remuneration Policy’s compliance with the 18 principles scheduled in SYSC 19B.1.5 to 19B.1.24 (inclusive) in a way which is appropriate to its size, internal organisation and the nature, scope and complexity of its activities.

These requirements apply to remuneration of any type paid by the Firm or from the AIFs it manages, including carried interest, as well as any transfer of units/shares of the AIF, made in exchange for professional services rendered by the AIFM to certain AIFM Remuneration Code Staff.

Firm’s AIFM Remuneration Code Staff are identified as follows: AIFM Remuneration Code Staff are defined as follows (as set out in SYSC 19B.1.3R): •

Senior Management;

•

Those individuals holding control functions;

•

Risk takers; and

•

Any employees receiving total remuneration that takes them into the same remuneration bracket as senior

management and risk takers. This Policy and the Remuneration Principles are primarily directed at AIFM Remuneration Code Staff. However, this Policy also sets out where these will be applied on a Firm-wide basis. The Firm will apply the AIFM Remuneration Code to all staff within the Firm.

The Firm’s Risk Management Alamut, its Partners and its staff (collectively, the “Firm”) has established a Remuneration Policy to promote sound and effective risk management and do not encourage risk taking which is inconsistent with the risk profiles, rules or instruments of the fund. The Firm has implemented policies, procedures and practices to identify, measure, manage and monitor risk. These are proportionate given the nature, scale and complexity of the Firm’s activities and its risk tolerance. The Firm’s risk management is detailed in the Firm’s Business Risk Framework and comprises: •

Definition of the Firm’s risk tolerance;

•

Risk identification;

•

Risk documentation;

•

Risk monitoring; and

•

Risk measurements.

Statement of Responsibilities The Governing Body of Alamut is responsible for approving and maintaining this Policy and overseeing the implementation of a robust remuneration policy, which will align the Firm’s remuneration practices with its risk tolerance. The Governing Body is responsible for the total process of risk management, which includes remuneration risk. The Firm’s Chief Operating Officer (“COO”) and Chief Investment Officer (“CIO”) set the risk profile and risk tolerance of the Firm, and its related policies and procedures. In addition, the Governing Body ensures that the Firm has implemented an effective, ongoing process to identify risk, to measure its potential impact against a broad set of assumptions and subsequently to ensure that such risks are actively managed. The COO will monitor the Firm’s Remuneration Policy in connection with its liquidity and capital requirements. The COO has the specific role of identifying, measuring and monitoring the risk profile of the Firm on a day-to-day basis and reporting to the Governing Body.

Remuneration Principles The Firm is required to comply with 9 Principles, in a way that is appropriate to the size, scope and complexity of the Firm. Given the size of the Firm, it can rely on proportionality to disapply certain aspects of the Remuneration Principles. These Principles are as follows: Principle 1 – Risk management and risk tolerance Principle 2 – Supporting business strategy, objectives, values and long-term interests of the firm Principle 3 – Governance Principle 4 – Control functions Principle 5 – Remuneration Structure Principle 6 – Variable Remuneration Adjusted to Account for Current Future Risks Principle 7 – Pension policy Principle 8 – Personal investment strategies Principle 9 – Avoidance of the remuneration code

Remuneration Principle 1 - Risk Management and Risk Tolerance The Firm, through its various policies and procedures, has aligned remuneration with effective risk management, over a multi-year framework. The Firm’s CIO and COO will ensure that such policies and procedures remain effective and align with the Firm’s business and risk strategy. The structure of the Firm’s remuneration policy is designed to ensure sound and effective risk management through: •

considering individual, team, and Firm performance;

•

alignment with the Firm’s business strategy, values and long term-goals;

•

alignment with the protection of the Firm’s clients, customers and investors;

•

ensuring variable remuneration does not undermine the viability and sustainability of the Firm and its ability to meet its capital and liquidity requirements;

•

considering the implications of awarding variable remuneration through a multi-year framework; and

•

ensuring financial and non-financial incentives do not promote excessive risk-taking.

The Firm applies this Principle on a Firm-wide basis.

Remuneration Principle 2 – Supports Business Strategy, Objectives, Values, Interests and avoids conflicts of Interest To support the Firm’s long-term business strategy, the Firm has adopted a top-down multi-year remuneration framework. This ensures that variable remuneration is only paid from risk adjusted profits based upon the performance of the business, the relevant business line and the individual and only after the Firm’s liquidity and capital requirements have been considered on a rolling three-year period. The Firm has also adopted policies and procedures aimed at mitigating any potential conflicts that may arise between staff members and the Firm, staff members and the AIF it manages and between one AIF and another. The Firm also maintains a Conflict of Interests Register which includes potential conflicts relating to remuneration, as well as the procedures the Firm has implemented to mitigate these conflicts. The Firm applies this Principle on a Firm-wide basis.

Remuneration Principle 3 - Governance The Governing Body has signed-off Statement of Responsibilities (in line with the Senior Managers and Certification Regime- “SMCR”) for each of the Firm’s senior managers, which set out the responsibilities allocated to them. Due to the size of the Firm, it does not consider it appropriate to have a separate remuneration committee. Instead, this function is undertaken by the Governing Body. This will be kept under review and should the need arise. The Firm will establish such a committee.

Remuneration Principle 4 – Control Functions The Governing Body meets at least annually to set the Firm’s Remuneration Policy for the following year. This includes a review of the Firm’s Remuneration Policy for the previous year and its performance and risk adjustments.

The Firm ensures that individuals performing relevant Certification functions or/and Senior Management Functions (“SMF”) remain independent from the business areas they oversee to avoid potential conflicts of interest. As the Firm is relatively small with a limited number of personnel it is inevitable that it will not always be possible to ensure independence. Where relevant this is referenced in the firm’s Conflicts of Interest Policy. The Governing Body determines the remuneration of all relevant members of staff (e.g. senior managers, risk takers, control functions, etc.) against specified objectives linked to the Firm’s adherence to its risk profile.

Remuneration Principle 5 - Remuneration Structure The Valuation Committee is responsible for conducting an on-going review of the valuation methodologies used in pricing securities to ensure that they are appropriate considering the circumstances at the time. The Valuation Committee reviews this Policy and the Firm’s valuation procedures considering regulatory, business or other applicable changes, such as where the Firm adopts a new investment strategy or invests in a new asset type not covered by the existing valuation guidelines. The Valuation Committee may approve exceptions to the Policy in certain cases. At least on a quarterly basis, the following procedures will be undertaken by the Valuation Committee: •

prices are compared between the Firm and Administrator, including a semi-annual review of the Administrator’s pricing procedures and adherence to the Valuation Policy;

•

prices are compared between different funds and managed accounts to ensure a consistent approach is used;

•

any discrepancies or differences are investigated, and exceptions are reported to the Valuation Committee, including noting single-source quote prices, illiquid exchange prices, and internally generated prices; and

•

any stale prices are reviewed.

Any proposed recommendations for change in the valuation policy or pricing methodology must be made to the Valuation Committee for its review and approval.

Remuneration Principle 6 – Variable Remuneration Adjusted to Account for Current Future Risks The Firm's risk analysis is incorporated into the Firm's Business Risk Framework and considers actual and potential risks faced by the Firm on an ongoing basis. The size of the Firm’s variable remuneration pool is based upon risk

adjusted profits, rather than revenues, and considers the risks identified in the Business Risk Framework and the cost of and requirement for capital in both the short and long term.

Remuneration Principle 7 – Pension Policy The Firm does not offer any non-cash pension benefits. The Firm provides pension contributions to all members of staff, as required by law.

Remuneration Principle 8 – Personal Investment Strategies Staff are required not to use personal hedging strategies or remuneration and liability-related insurance to undermine the risk alignment effects embedded in their remuneration arrangements. Any employee found to be in breach of these provisions will be required to repay any variable remuneration paid for the year and will be subject to disciplinary action.

Remuneration Principle 9 – Avoidance of the Remuneration Code The Firm’s remuneration policies and procedures are designed to ensure compliance with the Code. All variable remuneration is paid directly by the Firm or another group entity subject to the Code and agreed by the Governing Body on at least an annual basis.

Annex Appendix 1 - Assessment of Proportionality Elements

We have set out below our assessment of each of the elements and our conclusion on whether we will disapply the Pay-out Process Rules. We will review, and update, if necessary, this assessment on an annual basis.

policies.title: Trade Error Policy
policies.summary: Trade Error Policy Scope of Policy Alamut Investment Management LLP (“Alamut” or “Firm”) has put in place procedures, systems and training and other arrangements to ensure that trades are effected correctly and promptly, including a “four eyes” trade approval policy, together wit
policies.content:
Trade Error Policy

Trade Error Policy Scope of Policy Alamut Investment Management LLP (“Alamut” or “Firm”) has put in place procedures, systems and training and other arrangements to ensure that trades are effected correctly and promptly, including a “four eyes” trade approval policy, together with a commitment to maximising the degree of STP (straight through processing) and minimising the number of keystrokes (i.e. manual error potential). Despite such arrangements, trade errors may occur and Alamut has therefore adopted this policy for the purpose of ensuring that any such errors are handled appropriately. The following are examples of trade errors which could occur and should be handled with reference to this policy: •

The placement of orders (either buy or sell) in excess of the amount intended to trade

•

A sell trade when it should have been a buy, or vice versa

•

Any transaction in a security other than the intended one

•

A trade which is outside regulatory or fund restrictions (which may also be a “breach”)

•

Incorrect corporate action processing

•

Incorrect allocations

Alamut will use its best efforts to correct any trade error as soon as possible, in a manner that is in the best interests of its clients.

Counterparty Errors Where an error is caused in whole or in part by the broker or counterparty, the Firm will take steps to resolve the error with the broker or counterparty such that the Firm’s clients do not incur losses (or opportunity costs) relative to the transaction that the Firm had instructed. This may include reimbursement by the broker or counterparty of any resulting loss as appropriate under the circumstances. The broker or counterparty must not contribute to any loss arising solely from an error by Alamut staff.

Procedure In the event a trade error occurs, the following procedures will apply: •

Trade errors must be reported by staff immediately upon discovery to Tom Alcock, the Compliance Officer, as well as to the CIO Faiz Bhanji.

•

A plan to correct the error must be agreed to by the CO and CIO as soon as possible.

•

Trade errors must be corrected as soon as possible, in a manner intended to minimise any loss or potential loss to the clients, provided however that the individual responsible for committing and/or identifying the effort should generally not take remedial action before reporting the error to the CO and CIO and agreeing such remedial action with them, except for situations where a mistaken transaction can be reversed before execution and hence time is of the essence. •

In particular, the mistaken trades should not be allowed to “run” once identified in the hopes of subsequent price moves reducing or reversing the error, nor should mistaken positions be held solely in the hopes of liquidity or transaction levels improving, thereby exposing the clients to greater risk or for a longer time.

•

A written Trade Error Report must be completed, including the following: •

The circumstances leading to the error and precise details of the error – dealer, security, dates and times;

•

Original trade price and the price at the time of correction;

•

How the error was identified;

•

Details of who the error was reported to and when;

•

Remedial action taken;

•

Any profit or loss (or other non-financial consequences) arising from the remedial action taken; and

•

Approval of the adequacy of the remedial action taken by: 1) the portfolio manager; and 2) the Compliance Officer.

• •

Review of controls and processes in place to assess adequacy.

The Trade Error Report must be available for inspection by all investors, and should also be provided to the relevant Fund Directors (which may be at the next quarterly board meeting)

•

Details of all trade errors should also be reported to the Firm’s Governing Body which may be at the next formal meeting of such, except where the size or other circumstances of the error make it appropriate for it to be informed promptly.

Allocation of P&L from Errors

The Firm does not receive any third-party payments or non-monetary benefits for routing orders or from the trading venues so selected for the execution of orders (or, where relevant, from the execution venues selected) and nor does it impose any charges upon any other participants in a transaction. To the extent that any nonmonetary benefits are received the firm will ensure that: •

any such payments or non-monetary benefits will not impair the firm’s requirement to act in the best interests of its clients

•

any such payments or non-monetary benefits will conform to the regulatory requirements concerning ‘conflicts of interest’ and ‘inducements

•

clients are informed of such payments and non-monetary benefits

Further information about the Firm’s policies or arrangements and how they are reviewed will be provided upon request.

Records

The Trade Error Report and supporting documentation will be maintained by the Compliance Officer for a period of five years. Where appropriate, the error will also be recorded on the Breaches Register – e.g. if it caused a breach of investment restrictions.

policies.content:
Valuation Policy

Valuation Policy Introduction

The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive (the “AIFMD”) and FUND 3.9 require the Firm to establish, maintain, implement and review written policies and procedures that ensure a sound, transparent, and comprehensive process for the proper valuation of assets of the alternative investment fund managed by the AIFM. This Valuation Policy has been prepared by the Firm, reviewed and approved by the valuation committee established by the AIFM (the “Valuation Committee”) and by the board of directors of the AIF (the “Board”). This Policy aims to cover all material aspects of the valuation process that applies to the Alamut Master Fund Ltd., the “AIF” or the “Fund”). This Policy will be reviewed by the Valuation Committee whenever material changes are made to the Firm’s valuation process or, at a minimum, on an annual basis. The senior management of the Firm will have responsibility for ensuring adherence to this Valuation Policy. The Firm’s valuation principles, policy and procedures are designed to ensure that all investments are valued in accordance with the procedures described in the offering memorandum and articles of association of the AIF (collectively, “Fund Documents”), applicable law and accounting standards, and sound valuation practices. The valuation of AIF assets forms an integral part of the net asset value calculation (“NAV”) on the basis of which investors in the AIF subscribe and redeem on each Dealing Day. The NAV is also the basis on which the Management Fee and Performance Fee are calculated.

Valuation Responsibilities

Under the AIFMD, the proper and independent valuation of AIF assets is the responsibility of the AIFM. This includes the oversight of the entire valuation process as well as the resolution of pricing disputes. This section outlines the obligations, roles and responsibilities of all parties involved in the valuation process.

Valuation Committee

The Firm, with the consent of the Board, has established the Valuation Committee. The Valuation Committee will consist of at least one representatives of the Firm, and at least two representatives of the Board of the AIF, provided that each such Board representative is an independent director. The current members of the Valuation Committee are: Tom Alcock David Schofield Jim Kandunias The Valuation Committee carries out the valuation function within the Firm and is responsible for the design and implementation of the Firm’s valuation policies, approving valuation sources and methodology for any new asset types, and reviewing valuations of specific assets and applicable pricing methodology. The role of the Valuation Committee also includes advising the Board on valuation issues generally. The Firm and the Board have delegated full responsibility to the Valuation Committee on any re-valuations that may increase or decrease the asset value of the AIF in any NAV period and authorised the Valuation Committee to act as the final authority. A meeting of the Valuation Committee is quorate if three members of the Valuation Committee are present, and decisions are required to be unanimous. Other members of staff or advisers or third parties may attend meetings of the Valuation Committee from time to time with the permission of the Valuation Committee. The Valuation Committee will meet formally on an annual basis to discuss the implementation of this Policy and the functioning of the valuation process. As part of the meeting the following information will be provided: Fund valuation; details of pricing exceptions and proposed resolutions; details of stale prices and proposed pricing for these items; details of any illiquid investments and proposed pricing for these items; details of any side pockets and proposed pricing; and

new investment/instruments to be approved and proposed pricing methodology to be used (subject to New Investment Type Approval see below). Extraordinary meetings to handle an urgent issue, such as any valuation disputes, can occur between scheduled quarterly meeting dates; however, matters handled and agreed between scheduled meeting dates (whether in an extraordinary meeting or by some other means) will be ratified at the next scheduled Valuation Committee meeting.

Board of Directors

Under the Fund Documents and applicable law, the Board retains ultimate responsibility for ensuring the assets are valued in accordance with the Fund Documents and for the general oversight of the operations of the AIF. The Board is also responsible for appointment of service providers to the AIF, including the AIFM and the Administrator. These duties are independent from the responsibility of the Firm under the AIFMD for the proper and independent valuation of AIF assets. The Board will assist the Firm in defining the valuation policy of the AIF and oversee any updates and ad-hoc nonstandard valuation pricing proposals before they are implemented by the Firm and the Administrator. The estimated daily P&L and monthly published NAV and any equivalent reporting by the AIFM will be made available to the Board on request. In addition, the published monthly NAV will be reviewed at quarterly Board of Directors meetings. At the request of the Board, the AIFM or the Administrator will prepare reports that detail the relevant positions and the valuation methodology, and the Directors can enquire about the appropriateness of the pricing of such positions.

Administrator

Morgan Stanley Fund Services (Cayman) Limited has been appointed as the Administrator of the AIF. The Administrator has been engaged to perform certain administrative services on behalf of the AIFs, including: 1)

computation of a daily estimate and a published NAV for the AIF;

2)

accounting of all fund, security and financial transactions;

3)

application of pricing to the AIF’s portfolio in accordance with the agreed pricing sources;

4)

daily reconciliation of the AIF’s assets with custodians and prime brokers;

5)

calculation of Management Fee and Performance Fee (as defined in the Fund Documents); and

6)

investor accounting and reporting, including publication of NAV.

NAV calculation and reporting is performed by the Administrator and is overseen by the Firm. The Firm has agreed the policies and procedures to ensure the reasonableness of individual values with the Administrator. The Firm will aim to conduct an annual due diligence review of the Administrator’s policies and procedures to ensure they continue to meet the AIFMD requirements.

Depositary

Morgan Stanley Fund Services (“MSFS”) does not act as a full depositary for the purposes of AIFMD but will provide certain safekeeping, cash flow monitoring and oversight functions, covering the requirements specified in Article 21(7) (Cash Monitoring), 21(8)(b) (Ownership Verification) and 21(9) (Oversight) of the AIFMD. In respect of valuations, the role of MSFS is to oversee that the valuations are carried on in accordance with the applicable law, the AIF instruments of incorporation and the valuation policy of the Firm.

Auditor

E&Y (Cayman Islands) has been retained as the independent auditor of the AIFs. An annual report and audited financial statements will be sent to Investors within 90 days of the end of each AIF’s financial year, or as soon as reasonably practicable thereafter. The AIF prepares its financial statements in accordance with US Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (‘U.S. GAAP’). To the extent that U.S. GAAP would require any of the AIF’s assets or liabilities to be valued in a manner that differs from this Valuation Policy, as may be amended from time to time, the Board, in consultation with the Firm may value such assets or liabilities: (i) in accordance with U.S. GAAP, solely for purposes of preparing the U.S. GAAP-compliant annual audited financial statements, or (ii) in accordance with this Valuation Policy (without regard to any U.S GAAP requirements relating to the determination of fair value) for all other purposes. Non-compliance with U.S GAAP may result in the auditors issuing a qualified or an adverse opinion on the financial statements depending on the nature and level of materiality of the non-compliance.

Calculation and Reporting of NAV

NAV calculation and reporting to investors is performed by the Administrator and overseen by the Firm. Valuation will be performed in accordance with the terms of the Fund Documents, applicable law and U.S. GAAP. The NAV is calculated on the last business day of each calendar month and such other day or days as the Board may from time to time determine (each a “Valuation Day”). Cash and investment positions are confirmed and reconciled against external prime brokers. The Administrator provides a daily P&L estimate. A daily estimated NAV is calculated by the Firm with an official NAV reported monthly by the Administrator. Monthly NAV calculations are provided to the Firm within 10 business days of the valuation point and ‘signed-off’ by the Firm by way of an email or other electronic means to the Administrator. In the event that, after sign-off, an error is found to have occurred, the decision to re-state a NAV will be made by the Valuation Committee in consultation with the Administrator based on the specific circumstances and the materiality of the error. Any correction that would result in a movement in the NAV of (+/-) 50bps or greater would be deemed material.

Valuation Sources and Fair Valuation Hierarchy

The Administrator and the Firm obtain pricing services from a range of standard industry pricing providers (“Pricing Providers”) as set out in the Annex. The Firm’s general approach to pricing is to use multiple sources where available. Where appropriate, independent broker-dealer quotes may be used in pricing certain types of investments. As a general principle, independent broker-dealers who are active market makers or who trade such securities will be used.

The Valuation Committee will be notified of any changes to the Pricing Providers currently in use and any deviations in the use of such approved services for specific investments. As a further check on pricing, prices will be regularly reconciled to the Administrator’s pricing sources identified in the Annex. The Valuation Committee will be responsible for setting valuation guidelines for any new asset classes or strategies.

A fair value hierarchy of inputs is used in measuring fair value that maximises use of observable inputs and minimises use of unobservable inputs by requiring that the most observable inputs are used when available. The fair value hierarchy is categorised into three levels based on inputs as follows: •

Level 1 – Unadjusted quoted prices in active markets that are accessible at the measurement date for identical unrestricted assets or liabilities;

•

Level 2 – Quoted prices in markets that are not active or financial instruments for which all significant inputs are observable, either directly or indirectly, and;

•

Level 3 – Prices or valuations that require inputs that are both significant to the fair value measurement and unobservable.

When the inputs used to measure fair value fall into different levels of the fair value hierarchy, the level in the fair value hierarchy in which the asset or liability is assigned, will be determined based on the lowest level input. The Valuation Committee will have final say for fair value hierarchy assignment (subject to consent of the Board). Any change in fair value hierarchy will be reviewed and documented regularly by the Valuation Committee.

Valuation

All instruments are marked to market. The prices at which Designated Investments are marked are obtained wherever possible from consistent, reliable independent price providers or sources. The default valuation hierarchies for OTC and non-OTC instruments are defined in Appendix 1. The Fund and the AIFM will provide the Administrator with a framework to use to arrive at a price for NAV calculation purposes for certain instruments.

Validation and Controls

The Fund Administrator will complete a validation process to determine that the source to be used for valuation purposes is reasonable when compared to other sources. This is assessed by comparing the primary source to additional source(s) and validating that the pricing is within the defined tolerance thresholds. Appendix 2 describes

this process in more detail and defines the guideline tolerance thresholds. An additional comparison to the AIFM pricing can be performed if requested. The AIFM will review and sign off on the pricing to be used in the NAV. The AIFM is expected to raise challenges if any price has a large deviations from the AIFM valuation or if the AIFM consider that the fund valuation prices are not a good reflection of fair-value.

New Investment Type Approval

New investment type approval will be carried out by the Valuation Committee, subject to submission of the following information: details of the instrument/investment type; AIFs that the instrument/investment type is proposed to be used for; confirmation that the instrument/investment type is within the investment program of the Fund; proposed valuation methodology to be used; proposed pricing sources to be used; and start date for the new instrument/investment to be used.

Hard-to-Value Assets and Use of Models

The AIF generally invests in highly liquid instruments where a market price is readily available. As such, the AIFM does not currently utilise models in its valuations. If investments in more complex/harder to price instruments where a higher risk of inappropriate valuation exists are considered, this Valuation Policy will be updated, with additional valuation procedures put in place to ensure fair valuation of such investments. Where an investment is illiquid or a price is determined by the Valuation Committee using non observable market data, care will be taken to ensure that market events which could affect the valuation are monitored and that the valuation is reviewed as appropriate. Any such valuations will be monitored and reviewed periodically. All employees that are exposed to, or uncover, data or information which may call in to question the validity of such

valuations are obliged to inform the senior management, who can then refer the information to the Valuation Committee for proper consideration. Any decision to allow the side-pocketing of illiquid/hard-to-value positions will be taken only after careful consideration by the Valuation Committee in consultation with the Board. If the Board approves such a decision it should be ensured that side-pocket policies are clearly communicated to all investors. The criteria for sidepocketing individual positions should be as consistent as possible.

Stale prices

The price sources for certain types of positions might become obsolete or inaccurate with time. To safeguard from such events occurring, the Firm has put appropriate controls in place to assess the validity of the prices being used. A price testing exercise takes place on a regular basis whereby all price quotes provided from different sources are analysed and their validity questioned. Such analysis will, among other things, include: 1.

verifying values by a comparison amongst counterparty-sourced pricings and over time;

2.

validating values by comparison of realised prices with recent carrying values;

3.

considering the reputation, consistency and quality of the valuation source;

4.

a comparison with values generated by a third party;

5.

an examination and documentation of exemptions;

6.

highlighting and researching any differences that appear; and

7.

testing for stale prices and implied parameters.

In the case where a price source is no longer deemed accurate, the Firm will contact the Administrator and notify them of the need to substitute the specific price source with a more appropriate one. The Firm will provide the relevant supporting evidence to the Board and the Valuation Committee so that the update can be signed off.

Reviews of Valuation Policies and Procedures

The Valuation Committee is responsible for conducting an on-going review of the valuation methodologies used in pricing securities to ensure that they are appropriate considering the circumstances at the time. The Valuation Committee reviews this Policy and the Firm’s valuation procedures considering regulatory, business or other applicable changes, such as where the Firm adopts a new investment strategy or invests in a new asset type not covered by the existing valuation guidelines. The Valuation Committee may approve exceptions to the Policy in certain cases. At least on a quarterly basis, the following procedures will be undertaken by the Valuation Committee: •

prices are compared between the Firm and Administrator, including a semi-annual review of the Administrator’s pricing procedures and adherence to the Valuation Policy;

•

prices are compared between different funds and managed accounts to ensure a consistent approach is used;

•

any discrepancies or differences are investigated and exceptions are reported to the Valuation Committee, including noting single-source quote prices, illiquid exchange prices, and internallygenerated prices; and

•

any stale prices are reviewed.

Any proposed recommendations for change in the valuation policy or pricing methodology must be made to the Valuation Committee for its review and approval.

Valuation Disputes or Errors

The Firm and the Administrator have robust procedures in place to ensure the correct valuation is applied to the assets held by the AIF. Should a dispute or difference in valuation or an error be identified, the matter will be referred to the Valuation Committee which will convene as necessary to provide advice in relation to the valuation of a particular asset (or assets) or restating the NAV.

Any valuation proposal in relation to a particular asset (or assets) will be put to vote by the Valuation Committee. The Valuation Committee will be entitled to make a final determination. Once a valuation proposal is approved or ratified, it will be submitted to the Administrator for inclusion in the calculation of NAV. Any decision made on pricing will be recorded and retained for a minimum period of 5 years.

The asset price is selected based on all pricing sources using the hierarchy described in this section. A comparison price is selected based on remaining pricing sources that are closest to the primary source price.

Appendix 2 Price comparison process

This section covers all the checks performed to evaluate if the two independent prices are within tolerance. Comparison Framework: the difference between the primary source price and the comparison price is evaluated based on market value difference for the position, price difference, percentage price difference, sensitivity based difference (number of DV01, number of Credit DV01, Credit DV01 ratio, number of Delta, number of Vega difference). Thresholds for price variance are defined within the Administration Agreement. The AIFM reserves the right to overwrite these guidelines and deem difference as acceptable if price differences are within a bid/ask range observed in the market for same/similar positions.
